title: Will The Trump Administration Cause A Balance Of Payments Crisis In The US? | April 19, 2025

This is going to be another fun one. If
last week was the most important around
the horn presentation uh we've done, uh
this one will certainly be up there as
well. Uh so this week's key macro
question is in my opinion uh how
dangerous is the Trump administration's
attack on the Fed's independence. Our
answers are very very dangerous. The US
may be in the early innings of a balance
of payments crisis and President Trump's
attacks on the and Fed chair J Pal risks
exacerbating the geopolitically driven
supply demand imbalance in the Treasury
bond market. Reminder, the US has a net
international investment deficit worth
$24 trillion, more than twothirds our
GDP. Worse, we have twin fiscal and
current account deficits totaling minus
11% of GDP, demonstrating unprecedented
degrees of large s by the US private and
public sector.
The rest of the world was fine giving us
cash in exchange for IUS to live high on
the hog because of our economic
exceptionalism. But it cannot be
overstated that US exceptionalism was
primarily a function of that large s. If
you have a twin fiscal and current
account deficits totaling 11% of GDP, of
course you're going to grow faster than
everybody else.
Duh. Duh. Not everybody else can pull
that off. Um but anyway, so uh the uh
the moreover the US's exorbitant
privilege allowed us to finance that
large s at a cost of equity and debt
capital that simply does not make sense
when compared to peer economies that
share our precondition of bloated twin
deficits and a debt to liquidity ratio
north of 100%. Exorbitant privilege is
earned, not allocated, and can be eroded
by policy blunders like President Trump,
the Trump administration's Fisher Price
tariff formula, and or it could be
eroded by a disregard for the rule of
law that makes US assets exceptional to
begin with, such as nullifying court
rulings, however politicized they may
be. Just because you don't like it
doesn't mean you can nullify it, because
you're sending a signal that the rule of
law is being eroded to international
capital allocators. President Trump, if
you're listening, JP Pal knows this and
is doing what he must to preserve our
absurdly low cost of capital by doing
what every central bank would do to stem
capital outflows during a crisis of
confidence, implement hawkish policy.
The fact that bonds rallied during Pal's
incredibly hawkish speech is
confirmation of our view. These are the
economies that are going to go to that
negotiation in the White House and put
their feet on the table because I would
I'm going to say if you have you lost
your mind you not forget that we own X%
of your bond market you know have you lo
have if you and not just own X% of your
bond market because obviously the threat
of selling is is is is one dynamic that
we saw on April 9th and again on April
11th uh but another dynamic is they
could just stop giving us more money
right and force austerity by forcing our
a closure of our uh current account
deficit. Um you know so we have to be
careful you know the the folks on the
other side of the table have a lot of
negotiating leverage in my opinion I
think the capital account is is sacrosan
over the current account. President
Trump's focus dynamic were very focused
on the current account specifically
trade deficits. But in my opinion, I
think when you know when that Japanese
delegation showed up and you saw how
quick he was to celebrate the the part
the progress on the talks, I think you
know the Japanese um trade minister went

talking about right now in these in
these charts. Uh, US financial assets
are egregiously overvalued for any
scenario that features a persistent
erosion of exorbitant privilege. Let me
say that again. US financial assets are
egregiously overvalued for any scenario
that features a persistent erosion of
exorbitant privilege. So, right now, if
you look at the um so in this chart, we
show uh the 10-year nominal yield plus
the earnings yield for the respective

can normalize it. So it's essentially
the cost the aggregated cost of equity
and debt capital in the economy you know
it's approximate it's not not total
because obviously it's credit consumer
credit and corporate credit but it's a
approximate cost of equity and debt
capital uh across the economy so that we
can compare you know who's able to
finance its growth cheaper and more

now the US is basically tied with Japan
down at 8.68% 68% uh a combined, you
know, 10-year nominal treasury yield
plus the MSEI US index earnings yield.
Um you know, we're a twin deficit
economy, right? You know, we're the
we're the borrower economy. The borrower
the the other twin deficit economies, if
you look at India, it's 10.38%. So
essentially 200 basis points higher than
ours. If you look at the twin deficit UK
economy, it's 12.74%. So essentially 400
basis points higher than ours. And then
you look at Brazil, it's about uh

to 16 basis. That's a lot. Yeah. 1,600
basis points high now. I mean that's
that's this
is you know this is we could go from
here to somewhere over here overnight
just given this chart here. If if we
really had a serious capital outflow
problem stick that was you know
facilitated by a cascading sequence of
policy blockers. That is the risk. I'm
not saying it's the highest probability
outcome. That is the risk though. And so
I want investors to be aware that hey
we're financing our equity and debt
capital you know like we're a twin
surplus you know creditor economy but
we're not you know so that investors
need to be aware of that. Uh and so then

uh the sovereign fiscal balance to GDP
ratio for for those economies in the top
panel we show the current account uh uh
balance to GDP ratio in the middle panel
and then the twin surplus andor deficit
uh in this in this bottom panel. So, you
know, uh kind of just isolated the
current account. You know, we're we're
minus 4% current account, uh deficit to
GDP, worst in the world, at least
according to all these major economies.
The next closest one is, um Brazil and
and the UK at kind of 3%. Uh India's
minus 0.1%. And then you got Eurozone at
3%, China at 2%, Japan at 5%,
Switzerland at 5 6%. You know, we're
financing ourselves like we're the ones
giving everybody money. like the cost of
our equity and debt capital is is such
like we're the ones with all the money,
but we're not the ones with all the
money. We're the ones receiving the
money. And so, uh, that tells you that
there's, you know, that there's a
potential asset valuation mismatch if
those capital flows reverse. And that's
kind of one of the key takeaways from
from this presentation is that, you
know, if we go from a regime where
everyone's comfortable, you know, having
a significant chunk of their assets
parked in US dollars and US equities and
US stocks and US bonds and US, you know,
real estate and US private credit, US
private equity. You know, that stuff's
not all going to go to zero overnight.
Obviously, a lot of the fundamental
stories behind why you do that are still
intact. But all it takes is a change of
of of opinion and perception at the
margins, right? You know, the biggest
market moving events are when the whole
world thinks something is true and it
stops being true and that stuff comes
around every once in, you know, probably
10, 20 years. You know, these are the
kinds of things that happen like layman
going bankrupt and uh, you know, the the
the everyone remember when everyone
thought that the nation couldn't have a
a broad-based decline in home prices.
And so, we levered up our entire
mortgage back security sector and and do
a bunch of leverage and financial
products on top of that. And because we
all thought there was no way you could
possibly see a nationwide default
because prices can't go down nationwide.
Remember who everybody thought?
Everybody thought that by the way.
People thought that. Everyone thought
that otherwise we wouldn't have got
there. Well, my point is, you know,
everyone can think a lot of things until
until it changes. And so that's that's
the risk I'm trying to highlight. So
again, you know, we're twin deficit at -
11%. We're on par with Brazil at - 11%.
The next closest economy is the UK at
minus 8%, India minus 6% in terms of its
uh twin deficit. Again, we're on par
with Brazil in terms of our the sum the
aggregated sum of our sovereign fiscal
balance to GDP ratio of minus 7% and
current account deficit the GDP ratio of
minus of -4%. Yeah, we're on par with
Brazil. Brazil pays
24% to get equity and debt capital 24%.
Or the combined 24% rather sorry
combined 24%. We are paying a combined
8.68%.
And again, we have the same dynamics
from a twin deficit perspective. And so
to me, I think we have to be honest
about US exceptionalism. You know, this
is a public service announcement. We
have to be honest about why were we
exceptional? Are we exceptional because
we have a a sovereign fiscal balance
that is almost world beating at minus 7%
which is a record non-war non-recession
type number. Are we exceptional because
we are investing way more than we save
which is why our current account deficit
is 4%.
Are we exceptional because we have a
minus 11% twin deficit ratio or a twin
deficit uh to GDP ratio yet we're
financing ourselves like we're the ones
giving everyone the money as opposed to
ones receiving the money like Brazil and
in the UK and India. I surmise that this
might have been a contributing factor to
why we were exceptional and so the world
can take the exceptionalism away from us
and we need to be very cognizant of
that. Uh, contrary to the Trump
administration's narrative, the problem
is not surplus demand for the US dollar
due to its reserve currency status. It's
the US's abuse of exorbitant privilege.
Right? We don't, the world doesn't tell
us to overconume or overinvest or
overspend government-wise or over tax.
It's just happy to give us the money
because it keeps them gainfully employed
and they can build factories and hire
employed workers and it keeps them
employed, too. So everyone's winning in
that scenario. But we can't sit here and
say like, "Oh my god, they're because
the dollar is the reserve currency, it's
forcing us to have a twin deficit of
minus 11% the GDP." No, this is a
function of our inability to not live
high on the hog here as US consumers, as
US businesses, and a US government. We
are addicted to large S. That is the
American way. What does the rest of the
world think about us when you leave when
you go travel abroad? They think we're
all a bunch of fat, over consuming
people. And unfortunately that's true
for a lot of Americans and who who can't
get off the dole from a government
standpoint who who don't want to work as
hard as the rest of the world who who
don't want to uh uh uh you know save as
much as the rest of the world. So I
think we need to be more cognizant about
what's the order of operations here. uh
uh and you know I think Stephen Moran is
a brilliant man and obviously gets a lot
of stuff right but uh if he's of the
view that the uh US dollar uh is its
reserve currency status is the reason we
have you know a 7% deficit to GDP ratio
and an inability to you know to to cut
Medicaid and Medicare and social
security like that's not the reason the
reason is because we are living high on
the hog and we've gotten addicted to
fiscal and monetary larges we've gotten
addicted to not saving we've gotten
addicted to overinvesting we've gotten
addicted to over consuming. That's the
issue. The underlying issue is our abuse
of exorbitant privilege, not the reserve
currency status. We're abusing reserve
currency status. The US federal
government is on track to spend 1.1 to
1.2 trillion on net interest this year.
Can the US afford a Fed chair that is
not attuned to the risk of a reflexive
cycle of capital outflows and higher
bond
yields? I mean again we're about to we
spending net interest is $991 billion.
We know we got another hundredish
billion dollars coming as a function of
the refinancing that we need to do from
the lower interest rate regime to the
higher interest rate regime that we're
currently in. So you're talking about
somewhere between 1.1 and 1.2 trillion
dollars of just interest alone. Can we
really afford to have like our bond
yields run away from us? And they will
run away from us if the world is
concerned about central bank
independence.
If the world, you know, you saw the bond
market rallied when POW was being
hawkish. That is a telltale sign that
the concern is around the currency in
the bond market, you know, and so in my
opinion, this is the kind of stuff you
only see in emerging market balance
payments crisis. You know, I guess I've
lived long enough to see, you know,
elements of that here in in the US. Uh
and so we got to be very careful about
you know our perception of around
privilege and US exceptionalism and
what's driving what and and what the
ultimate problem you know the the
ultimate ill is and the ultimate problem
is you know there's people in and around
the administration that think it's the
dollar's reserve uh currency status in
my opinion based on the analysis that we
just presented here today I think it's
pretty clearly a function of our you
know living high on the hog and our
addiction to large s uh from a lifestyle
perspective here in the that the rest of
the world was happy to finance because
it gave them jobs. But if we, you know,
they you could have a very severe
mutually as true of destruction dynamic
occur uh if if uh if if we try to bully
our way into into a different paradigm.
